metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether `fetcher` and `run_differs` should use `project_default='DIG'` or keep their current `project_default=''` (config-only) when the jira factory builds the shared transport — document the choice explicitly in the factory if homogenizing, or preserve per-site defaults in the factory signature.
evidence: fetcher.py:371: `_s = acli_subprocess.resolve_jira_settings()` — no project_default
run_differs.py:402: `_s = acli_subprocess.resolve_jira_settings()` — no project_default
applier.py:604: `_s = acli_subprocess.resolve_jira_settings(project_default='DIG')` — explicit DIG
_attestation.py:65: `settings = acli_subprocess.resolve_jira_settings(project_default='DIG')` — explicit DIG
Plan claim: 'behaviour-preserving change proven by the full reconcile suite staying green'
findings.finding: The plan states the jira factory builds AcliClient with `resolve_jira_settings(project_default='DIG')` for every consumer (a single uniform construction), claiming this is behaviour-preserving — but `fetcher.py:371` and `run_differs.py:403` currently call `resolve_jira_settings()` with NO `project_default`, giving an empty-string fallback when JIRA_PROJECT is unset. Forcing `project_default='DIG'` on those two sites silently changes the project fallback for fetch and differ operations, which is a golden-hammer application of the applier/attestation construction form across all four sites without accounting for the two that intentionally use the config-only path.
findings.location: Scope section — 'The jira factory builds the AcliClient with resolve_jira_settings(project_default="DIG") so the transport carries the project for every consumer'; AC bullet for run_differs and _attestation
scenarios: A deployment where JIRA_PROJECT is unset in env and not in rebar.toml (e.g. a dev/CI environment relying on per-call defaults): fetcher and run_differs would silently start querying project 'DIG' instead of raising/failing-open, masking a misconfiguration.
A team whose Jira project is NOT 'DIG': after this change the fetcher would silently query the wrong project when JIRA_PROJECT is absent.
findings.suggested_fix: Give the jira factory an optional `project_default: str = ''` parameter and pass `project_default='DIG'` only at the two sites that currently use it (`applier._apply_batch`, `_attestation.build_acli_client_from_env`), preserving empty-string semantics for `fetcher` and `run_differs`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly address the project_default divergence between the four sites: either document why uniform `project_default="DIG"` is safe for `fetcher` and `run_differs` (e.g. their JQL already scopes to `_s.project` and an empty project causes its own failure), or build the per-site transport with per-site `project_default` values preserved (e.g. the fetcher's transport uses no default, matching current behavior).
evidence: fetcher.py:371: `_s = acli_subprocess.resolve_jira_settings()` — no project_default, empty-string fallback
run_differs.py:402: `_s = acli_subprocess.resolve_jira_settings()` — no project_default, empty-string fallback
applier.py:604: `_s = acli_subprocess.resolve_jira_settings(project_default='DIG')` — explicit DIG
_attestation.py:65: `settings = acli_subprocess.resolve_jira_settings(project_default='DIG')` — explicit DIG
Plan claim: 'behaviour-preserving change proven by the full reconcile suite staying green' without acknowledging per-site project_default divergence
findings.finding: The plan applies `resolve_jira_settings(project_default="DIG")` uniformly to ALL four transport-construction sites via the shared backend transport, but `fetcher.py:371` and `run_differs.py:402` currently call `resolve_jira_settings()` with NO project_default (empty-string fallback). Routing both through the single shared transport silently changes the project fallback for fetch and outbound-diff operations from '' (empty/config-only) to 'DIG', which is a behavioral change the plan labels as 'behaviour-preserving' without explaining why this delta is safe for those two sites.
findings.location: Scope section — 'The jira factory builds the AcliClient with resolve_jira_settings(project_default="DIG") so the transport carries the project for every consumer'; also AC bullets for run_differs.py and fetcher _load_acli
scenarios: A team whose Jira project is NOT 'DIG' and who relies on env/config for project resolution: after migration, fetcher and run_differs silently use 'DIG' as the project fallback instead of the prior empty-string (config-only) path, potentially causing JQL queries to target the wrong project.

A CI environment where JIRA_PROJECT is unset and rebar.toml has no jira.project: previously fetcher would get project='' (fail open); after migration it gets project='DIG', silently querying the wrong project.

scenarios: A reviewer or merge-commit reader sees the title and marks the ADF-sizing relocation as complete, skipping the follow-up story.
An implementer tries to deliver the title's promise and inadvertently re-introduces the deferred scope into this PR.

evidence: 'preserving fail-fast missing-env behaviour' — stated as a property with no test citation or command that withholds env vars and asserts a non-zero exit / exception
'grep 'AcliClient(' _attestation.py returns zero' — proves removal of old construction but not correctness of new path under missing-env conditions

findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC 4: `_attestation.build_acli_client_from_env()` fail-fast env behaviour
scenarios: The new backend-delegated path silently swallows a missing env var (e.g. returns None transport instead of raising); the grep-only AC passes but the fail-fast guarantee is broken in production.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or extend the applier/fetcher AC) that explicitly verifies the test-double migration — e.g. 'grep -rn "def _load_acli" tests/ | wc -l equals 0' or a dedicated lint rule that no test file returns a module-shaped object from _load_acli.
evidence: Scope: '~34 _load_acli test doubles migrated module-shaped → transport-shaped (assertions unchanged)'

AC (applier/fetcher): 'no seam double remains module-shaped — verified by make test green' — this phrase appears in the AC body but the verification method is 'make test green', not a targeted assertion
findings.finding: The ~34 _load_acli test-double migration (module-shaped → transport-shaped) is an explicitly described deliverable in Scope (landed) but has no dedicated AC. The only coverage is the indirect 'verified by make test green' within the applier/fetcher AC, which does not state what property is being verified (shape of return value, number of doubles migrated, etc.).
findings.location: Scope (landed) last bullet vs. AC for applier/fetcher
scenarios: A reviewer cannot confirm all 34 doubles were migrated without manually inspecting test files.
If a double is accidentally left module-shaped, make test green may still pass because the double silently returns the wrong type but existing assertions don't exercise the difference.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the AC 1 proving command with a self-enforcing cardinality check, e.g. `[ $(grep -c '"jira"' apply_inbound_records.py) -eq 1 ]` (exits non-zero if count ≠ 1), or rely solely on the named neutrality-gate test and update the AC text to reference it.
evidence: 'grep -n '"jira"' apply_inbound_records.py returns exactly one line'
Taxonomy item 5: missing cardinality assertion when the criterion names a count — the grep command does not produce a machine-checkable count of 1.
findings.finding: AC 1's proving command (`grep -n '"jira"' apply_inbound_records.py`) prints matching lines with line numbers but does not self-enforce the claimed cardinality of exactly one; a human must visually count, meaning CI cannot fail automatically if a second `"jira"` token is accidentally re-introduced.
scenarios: A future commit accidentally reintroduces a second `"jira"` literal; the `-n` grep still exits 0 and prints two lines, but the AC reads as 'returned lines' rather than failing CI.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `grep -n '"jira"' apply_inbound_records.py` with `[ $(grep -c '"jira"' apply_inbound_records.py) -eq 1 ] && echo PASS || echo FAIL` so the command exits non-zero on any count other than 1.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit back-out note: either a one-liner revert strategy (e.g. 'revert the single commit touching _load_acli + test doubles') or an expand-contract step (keep old module-shaped return alongside new transport-shaped return until all consumers are migrated, then remove the shim).
evidence: '~34 _load_acli test doubles migrated module-shaped → transport-shaped'
'behaviour-preserving change proven by the full reconcile suite staying green'
No mention of a revert strategy, feature flag, or expand-contract sequence anywhere in the plan
findings.finding: The _load_acli seam-shape change (module-shaped → transport-shaped) is a breaking interface change for all 34 consuming test doubles, yet the plan provides no rollback/back-out procedure. The plan describes it as 'behaviour-preserving' and relies solely on 'make test green' as the safety net, but T4 requires an explicit rollback mechanism, not just acknowledgement of the break.
scenarios: If a post-merge regression is discovered (e.g. a test double missed, an untested integration path broken), there is no documented rollback path — the team must reconstruct one ad hoc.
The change is merged and a downstream consumer (not covered by the reconcile suite) breaks; without a rollback plan the fix timeline is unclear.
findings.suggested_fix: Because all 34 doubles are migrated atomically in one story, the simplest rollback is a single git revert of the story commit; document this explicitly in the Testing section: 'Back-out: git revert <story-commit> restores all 34 doubles and the four transport sites atomically.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add or update an ADR / AGENTS.md / design-doc entry capturing the Backend port contract (what `transport` carries, how `vendor` flows, how to register a new backend) so future maintainers can extend without archaeology.
evidence: Absence: no ADR, AGENTS.md update, or design-doc entry is mentioned anywhere in the plan
'The backend-neutral core still named Jira concretely … This story routes the transport-construction sites through the Backend port' — a new architectural contract with no recorded rationale
The scope-split note (rebar:4af8-2d88-719e-4478) records WHAT was deferred but not WHERE the architecture is documented
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel architectural pattern — all transport construction routed through the Backend port, with provider identity flowing from `backend.vendor` — but includes no mention of capturing this decision in an ADR, AGENTS.md, or design-doc update. Future maintainers have no durable record of why the port boundary was drawn this way, what the contract is, or how to add a new backend.
findings.location: Scope (landed) section / AC block — no documentation artifact referenced
scenarios: A developer adding a second backend (e.g., Linear) must reverse-engineer the port contract from code rather than from a design doc
A reviewer of the follow-up DI story has no reference document explaining the Backend port's intended consumer surface
findings.suggested_fix: Add a short ADR (or an `## Architecture` section to AGENTS.md) with: (1) the Backend port interface definition, (2) the rule that all transport construction must go through `select_backend(config).transport`, (3) the `vendor` string convention, and (4) a pointer to the follow-up DI story for importer-site routing.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Revise the plan title to reflect the landed scope only (e.g. 'Route the 4 AcliClient transport-construction sites through the Backend port and de-hardcode provider-identity literals'), or add a one-line parenthetical '(transport sites only; vendor-importer routing deferred to rebar:4af8-2d88-719e-4478)' so the title is self-consistent with the landed AC set.

Scope (landed) enumerates only the 4 transport sites and provider-identity literals — vendor-importing modules and ADF-sizing relocation are absent

findings.location: Title / Scope (landed) section / AC section ([SPLIT → rebar:4af8-2d88-719e-4478] lines)
scenarios: An agent executing from the title alone would attempt to also route vendor-importing modules and relocate constants, producing scope drift beyond what the body intends.
A reviewer scanning only the title for completeness would flag the plan as incomplete, creating unnecessary back-and-forth.
findings.suggested_fix: Change the title to: 'Route the 4 AcliClient transport-construction sites through the Backend port; de-hardcode provider-identity literals (vendor-importer routing and constant relocation split to rebar:4af8-2d88-719e-4478)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least one in-process integration test (no network, but using the real jira factory and `select_backend` dispatch) that calls `select_backend(load_config()).transport` with a controlled config and asserts the returned object is a correctly-wired `AcliClient`, so the factory chain is exercised and not only grep-verified.
evidence: "~34 `_load_acli` test doubles migrated module-shaped → transport-shaped" — fakes are injected AT the seam, so `select_backend(load_config()).transport` is never called in any test
"Integration: the full reconcile unit+integration suite stays green" — green is achievable via transport-shaped fakes that entirely bypass the factory chain

"The jira factory builds the `AcliClient` with `resolve_jira_settings(project_default=\"DIG\")` so the transport carries the project for every consumer" — this factory invocation is not exercised by any described test
Neutrality gate uses source-grep (`AcliClient(` absent) to verify routing, not actual invocation of the new path
ABSENCE: no described test calls `select_backend(load_config()).transport` in a live or in-process-real-factory mode
findings.finding: The newly defaulted transport path — `select_backend(load_config()).transport` → jira factory → `AcliClient(resolve_jira_settings(project_default="DIG"))` — is never exercised by any described test. All ~34 test doubles are swapped at the `_load_acli` seam, bypassing the entire backend-selection→factory→client chain. The suite goes green (and the ACs are satisfiable) even if the factory wiring, env-var handling inside `resolve_jira_settings`, or the port dispatch in `select_backend` is silently broken.
scenarios: The jira factory's `resolve_jira_settings(project_default="DIG")` signature or env-var contract changes silently — all suite tests remain green because fakes bypass it
The `select_backend` dispatch condition has a bug for the jira key — still green via fakes at the seam
The `.transport` property on the backend port object has a wrong return type — grep-based AC passes, downstream fakes hide the mismatch until production
findings.suggested_fix: In the test suite, add a test that (1) sets the required env vars to minimal test values, (2) calls `select_backend(load_config()).transport` directly (or via `build_acli_client_from_env`), and (3) asserts the returned object is an `AcliClient` instance with the expected project (`DIG`). This exercises the `resolve_jira_settings` call, the port dispatch, and the `.transport` property without requiring a live Jira network connection, satisfying the proxy-validation concern.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm that S2 (ce4d) explicitly specifies `.transport` as a named accessor property on the `Backend` facade and `.vendor` as a named string attribute on `JiraBackend` (or update this plan to match whatever attribute names S2 actually lands).
evidence: Plan text: 'routes its outbound-diff client through `select_backend(config).transport`' and 'the selected backend's `vendor`'
grep for '.transport' in src/ returns no matches
grep for '.vendor' in src/ returns no matches
S2 plan text names the protocol 'TicketTransport' but does not define a `.transport` accessor property on the Backend facade
S2 plan text mentions RemoteRef(vendor=...) but does not specify a `.vendor` string attribute on Backend or JiraBackend
S3 plan text describes 'a Backend whose TicketTransport is an AcliClient' — using the protocol name, not a .transport accessor
findings.finding: The plan asserts `select_backend(load_config()).transport` and `backend.vendor` as the concrete attribute names on Backend/JiraBackend that this story's call sites will use, but neither `.transport` nor `.vendor` appears in the S2 (ce4d) prerequisite plan's spec text. S2 names the protocol 'TicketTransport' and defines `RemoteRef(vendor=...)`, but does not explicitly specify a `.transport` accessor property on the `Backend` facade or a `.vendor` string attribute on `JiraBackend`. The plan under review is therefore asserting a specific interface contract that the cited prerequisite does not confirm.
findings.location: Scope (landed) section: 'obtain the transport from the selected backend' and AC lines for run_differs.py / _attestation.py / apply_inbound_records.py
scenarios: If JiraBackend exposes the transport as .ticket_transport or via the Protocol directly (e.g. backend.ticket_transport) rather than .transport, every call site in this story's scope would fail at runtime.

If backend.vendor is not a named attribute (e.g. it's encoded in RemoteRef's vendor field rather than a top-level property), the apply_inbound_records.py de-hardcoding would require a different access path.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a cross-reference to the exact attribute names in S2's AC or file-impact section — e.g., 'Backend.transport: TicketTransport' and 'Backend.vendor: str' — or update this plan's call sites once S2's interface is final.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate and update all three `_fakes.install()` call sites (`test_roundtrip.py:123`, `test_jira_fixtures.py:60`, `test_snapshot_contract.py:90`) — either migrate them to the new transport-injection seam alongside `_fakes.py`, or confirm their seam survives unchanged and document why.
evidence: Plan says 'the integration `_fakes.py` double' is migrated, but does not name its three callers.
`_fakes.install()` implementation: `monkeypatch.setattr(fetcher_mod, "_load_acli", lambda: _FakeAcliModule(fixture_dir))`
After this story, `fetcher._load_acli` no longer exists as a patchable seam — three callers of `install()` go dark.
findings.finding: Three integration-test files that call `_fakes.install(monkeypatch, fetcher)` are unenumerated update targets: `test_roundtrip.py:123`, `test_jira_fixtures.py:60`, and `test_snapshot_contract.py:90` each patch `fetcher._load_acli` via `install()`. After this story removes the `_load_acli` seam from `fetcher` (replacing it with `select_backend(load_config()).transport`), patching `fetcher._load_acli` becomes a no-op and these three tests would silently lose their fake transport, causing them to attempt real Jira connections or fail with missing-env errors.
findings.location: Scope (landed) — '~34 `_load_acli` test doubles migrated module-shaped → transport-shaped'; tests/integration/rebar_reconciler/jira_contract/_fakes.py:82-90; test_roundtrip.py:123; test_jira_fixtures.py:60; test_snapshot_contract.py:90
scenarios: CI runs the integration suite; the three tests that call `_fakes.install()` pass their fake through a removed seam and end up exercising real Jira transport, failing on missing credentials.
Worse: if the `select_backend` call succeeds via env config, the tests run against a real backend instead of fixtures, producing non-deterministic results.
findings.suggested_fix: Update `_fakes.install()` to patch the new transport-injection point (e.g. `select_backend` or the backend registry) instead of `fetcher._load_acli`, and add the three caller files to the plan's edit-set.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the scope split with the parent epic (bbf1): either (a) add rebar:4af8-2d88-719e-4478 as a new S4b child story to the epic and update the epic's AC3 neutrality-gate count to reflect the two-part delivery, OR (b) expand this leaf to cover all 12 sites as the parent's S4 originally specified — the parent's plan is authoritative and the leaf may not silently route out-of-scope work to an untracked ticket.
evidence: Parent epic S4: 'route every core consumer through the Backend port: the 8 confirmed vendor-importing core modules AND the 4 transport/construction sites; move ADF-sizing + lossy-status/parent + duplicated maps/link-constants into adapters/jira/'

Parent epic AC3: 'After S4 the backend-neutral core no longer imports adapters.jira.* or the root vendor modules — enforced by a neutrality gate on the 12 rewired core sites (the 8 vendor-importing modules + the 4 transport/construction sites)'

Leaf plan 'Scope split' section: 'Split to follow-up [rebar:4af8-2d88-719e-4478]: routing the core importer sites (outbound_differ/inbound_differ/apply_inbound/outbound_comments/inbound_translate) off the vendor mappers… and single-sourcing the leak-fix constants under adapters/jira/'
Ticket 4af8-2d88-719e-4478 is absent from the epic's child decomposition list (S1=ef33, S2=ce4d, S3=94f9, S4=6673, S5=dfb9)
findings.finding: The leaf routes the deferred importer-site rewiring (8 vendor-importing core modules) and ADF-sizing/maps/link-constant relocation to a follow-up ticket (rebar:4af8-2d88-719e-4478) that does not exist in the parent epic's S1–S5 child decomposition, stepping outside the parent's plan; the parent's S4 AC requires a neutrality gate over all 12 rewired core sites, which the leaf's gate covers only partially (the 4 transport sites + apply_inbound_records).
findings.location: Scope split section; AC line '[SPLIT → rebar:4af8-2d88-719e-4478]'; parent epic bbf1-82e1-cf9d-494a S4 description and AC3
scenarios: After this story lands, the parent epic's S4 neutrality gate AC (12 sites) will remain unmet, potentially blocking the S5 physical relocation and the epic's overall 'core no longer imports vendor modules' guarantee.
The follow-up ticket 4af8-2d88-719e-4478 lives outside the epic's tracked decomposition, meaning it could be dropped or slip without the epic's completion criteria catching it.
findings.suggested_fix: Update the parent epic (bbf1-82e1-cf9d-494a) to register 4af8-2d88-719e-4478 as a new child story (S4b) in its decomposition, adjust its AC3 to say 'After S4a+S4b…', and re-review the epic. Only after the parent's plan is updated does the leaf's narrowed scope become a valid consistent narrowing rather than a divergence.

findings.finding: The plan's 'Confirmed leak sites' entry for outbound_differ.py:63 names only `_map_local_to_jira_fields` and `_diff_fields` as the imported vendor symbols, but the actual import block at that line also imports `_LOCAL_TO_JIRA_TYPE`, `_assignee_matches`, and `_extract_jira_field` from `rebar_reconciler.outbound_fields`. These three additional symbols are also vendor-specific outbound_fields names in a core module. The plan provides no rationale for excluding them from the rewire scope, yet AC1's neutrality grep (`none of the 12 rewired core modules import … root vendor modules`) would catch them — so either they are implicitly in scope (and the task is underspecified) or they are intentionally excluded (and the plan needs a stated rationale).
findings.suggested_fix: Add a parenthetical in the outbound_differ.py:63 leak-site entry: '— `from rebar_reconciler.outbound_fields import _map_local_to_jira_fields, _diff_fields` (plus `_LOCAL_TO_JIRA_TYPE`, `_assignee_matches`, `_extract_jira_field` re-exported for tests via `noqa: F401` — these are also vendor symbols; route all five through the Backend mapper or document the intentional exclusion with a rationale).'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
